---
title: "Homework 3"
format: pdf
editor: visual
author: "gabriela davidson"
date: "4-26-2026"
---

# Set up

```{r}
#| label: set_up
#| message: false

# read in tidyverse for data wrangling and visualization
library(tidyverse)
# read in here for reproducible file paths
library(here)
# read in janitor for cleaning column names
library(janitor)

# read in kelp data from data folder
kelp <- read_csv(here("data", "temp-kelp.csv"))

# read in personal data from data folder
my_data <- read_csv(here("data", "MyData_Homework3.csv"))
```

# Problem 1: Giant kelp fronds

## 1a. An appropriate test

The two appropriate tests to determine the strength of the relationship between ocean temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate are the Pearson correlation coefficient and the Spearman rank correlation coefficient. The Pearson correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two continuous variables and assumes that both variables are normally distributed. The Spearman rank correlation is a non-parametric alternative that measures the strength of a monotonic relationship between ocean temperature and elongation rate without assuming normality, making it appropriate if the normality assumption for Pearson is violated.

## 1b. Create a visualization

```{r}
#| label: kelp_visualization

# create scatterplot of ocean temperature vs kelp frond elongation rate
ggplot(data = kelp, # use kelp data frame
       aes(x = temp_c, # ocean temperature on x-axis
           y = kelp_elong)) + # elongation rate on y-axis
  geom_point(color = "darkgreen", # non-default point color
             size = 3,
             alpha = 0.7) + # slight transparency on points
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", # add linear trend line
              color = "darkorange", # non-default line color
              fill = "lightyellow", # confidence interval fill color
              se = TRUE) + # show confidence interval around line
  labs(x = "Ocean Temperature (°C)", # label x-axis with units
       y = "Kelp Frond Elongation Rate (cm day⁻¹)", # label y-axis with units
       title = "Relationship between ocean temperature and kelp frond elongation rate") +
  theme_bw() + # non-default theme
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) # remove minor gridlines to reduce clutter
```

## 1c.Check your assumptions and run your test

```{r}

# check normality of ocean temperature using a QQ plot
ggplot(data = kelp,
       aes(sample = temp_c)) + # check temperature variable
  geom_qq() + # plot sample quantiles against theoretical quantiles
  geom_qq_line(color = "blue") + # add reference line for normality
  labs(title = "QQ plot: Ocean Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_bw() # non-default theme

# check normality of kelp elongation rate using a QQ plot
ggplot(data = kelp,
       aes(sample = kelp_elong)) + # check elongation rate variable
  geom_qq() + # plot sample quantiles against theoretical quantiles
  geom_qq_line(color = "blue") + # add reference line for normality
  labs(title = "QQ plot: Kelp Frond Elongation Rate (cm day⁻¹)") +
  theme_bw() # non-default theme

# run Pearson correlation test between ocean temperature and kelp elongation rate
cor.test(kelp$temp_c, # predictor variable: ocean temperature
         kelp$kelp_elong, # response variable: kelp frond elongation rate
         method = "pearson") # specify Pearson method for linear relationship
```

## 1d.

To evaluate the strength of the relationship between ocean temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate, I used a Pearson correlation coefficient because both variables are continuous and approximately normally distributed, and the scatterplot suggested a linear relationship between them.
Ocean temperature was significantly negatively correlated with giant kelp frond elongation rate, meaning that kelp growing in warmer water tended to have slower growth rates (Pearson's r = -0.69, t(30) = -5.19, p < 0.001). This suggests that as ocean temperatures increase due to climate change, we would expect giant kelp fronds to grow more slowly, which could have important consequences for the overall health and structure of kelp forest ecosystems.

## 1e. Test implications
Our results indicate that higher ocean temperatures are associated with significantly lower giant kelp frond elongation rates, suggesting that ongoing ocean warming due to climate change may slow kelp growth. This is critical for our team to understand because reduced growth rates could diminish kelp forest biomass and structural complexity over time, threatening the many species that depend on these ecosystems. As ocean temperatures continue to rise, we should prioritize monitoring kelp growth rates in warmer regions as early indicators of broader kelp forest decline.

## 1f. Double check your own work
```{r}
#| label: kelp_spearman
# run Spearman rank correlation as the alternative non-parametric test
# does not assume normality, measures monotonic relationship
cor.test(kelp$temp_c, # predictor variable: ocean temperature
         kelp$kelp_elong, # response variable: kelp frond elongation rate
         method = "spearman") # specify Spearman method
```

Both the Pearson correlation (r = -0.69, t(30) = -5.19, p < 0.001) and the Spearman rank correlation (rho = -0.69, S = 9216.1, p < 0.001) led to the same decision to reject the null hypothesis that there is no relationship between ocean temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate. Both tests revealed a strong negative relationship of nearly identical magnitude, meaning that both would lead to the same interpretation that higher ocean temperatures are associated with lower kelp frond elongation rates. The close agreement between the two tests strengthens confidence in the conclusion, as the Pearson result indicates the relationship is linear while the Spearman result confirms it is also monotonic.

# Problem 2. Personal Data 

## 2a. Updating visualizations

```{r}
#| label: personal_data_summary
# summarize mean focus level and SD by work day
my_data_summary <- my_data |>
  group_by(work_day) |> # group by whether it was a work day
  summarize(
    mean_focus = mean(focus_level), # calculate mean focus level per group
    sd_focus = sd(focus_level) # calculate standard deviation per group
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: personal_data_plot_1

# plot focus level by work day with raw data, mean, and error bars
ggplot() +
  geom_jitter(data = my_data, # plot raw individual data points
              aes(x = work_day,
                  y = focus_level,
                  color = work_day),
              width = 0.1, # jitter horizontally only
              height = 0,
              size = 3,
              alpha = 0.7) + # slight transparency
  geom_point(data = my_data_summary, # plot group mean as larger point
             aes(x = work_day,
                 y = mean_focus,
                 color = work_day),
             size = 5) +
  geom_errorbar(data = my_data_summary, # plot SD as error bars
                aes(x = work_day,
                    ymin = mean_focus - sd_focus, # lower bound of SD
                    ymax = mean_focus + sd_focus, # upper bound of SD
                    color = work_day),
                width = 0.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("yes" = "#D55E00", # non-default colors
                                "no" = "#0072B2")) +
  labs(x = "Work Day (yes/no)", # label x-axis
       y = "Focus Level (1-5)", # label y-axis with units
       subtitle = paste("Most recent observation:", max(my_data$date))) + # most recent date
  theme_bw() + # non-default theme
  theme(legend.position = "none", # remove redundant legend
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) # reduce visual clutter
```


```{r}

# plot water intake vs focus level as scatterplot with trend line
ggplot(data = my_data,
       aes(x = water_intake_L, # water intake on x-axis
           y = focus_level)) + # focus level on y-axis
  geom_point(color = "#009E73", # non-default color
             size = 3,
             alpha = 0.7) + # slight transparency
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", # add linear trend line
              color = "gray40", # non-default line color
              se = TRUE) + # show confidence interval
  labs(x = "Water Intake (liters)", # label x-axis with units
       y = "Focus Level (1-5)", # label y-axis with units
       subtitle = paste("Most recent observation:", max(my_data$date))) + # most recent date
  theme_bw() + # non-default theme
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) # reduce visual clutter
```

## 2b. Captions

Figure 1. Focus level (1–5 scale) on work days versus non-work days, based on 13 days of personal observations. Points show individual daily observations, larger points show group means, and error bars represent ± 1 standard deviation. Focus level was slightly lower on work days compared to non-work days, with greater variability on work days.
Figure 2. Relationship between daily water intake (liters) and focus level (1–5 scale) across 13 days of personal observation. Each point represents one day. A positive association between water intake and focus level is visible, suggesting that higher water intake may be associated with higher focus, though the sample size is small and this pattern should be interpreted cautiously.

# Problem 3. Affective visualization

## 3a. Description
An affective visualization for my personal data could take the form of a hand-drawn weekly calendar where each day is represented as a water droplet shape, with the size of the droplet corresponding to how much water I drank that day and the color (ranging from pale yellow to deep blue) representing my focus level for that day. Days where I worked could be marked with a small sun icon, while rest days could have a moon. This format would make the data feel personal and embodied — the viewer would literally see how "full" or "energized" each day felt, connecting hydration and mental energy in an intuitive visual language that goes beyond what a standard chart could convey.

## 3b. Sketch
![Initial Sketch for drawing](../sketch.png)

## 3c. Draft 
![Initial Sketch for drawing](../draft.png)

## 3d. Artist Statement
Content: This piece shows 13 days of personal observations tracking daily water intake and focus level, with additional variables including number of study sessions and whether the day was a work day or rest day, exploring whether staying hydrated is associated with feeling more mentally focused.
Influences: I was directly influenced by Stefanie Posavec and Giorgia Lupi's Dear Data project, which encodes personal behavioral data into dense hand-drawn postcards using repeated simple shapes with a legend, making data feel intimate and personal rather than clinical.
Form: The piece is a hand-drawn postcard created with colored pencils and pen on paper, designed to be small and personal in the style of Dear Data.
Process: I first mapped each variable to a visual element — water intake to fill level, focus to color, study sessions to bubbles, and work day to sun or moon symbols. I then sketched all 13 glasses in pencil, added the water fill and color for each day using the data table, drew the bubbles and symbols, and finished with a hand-drawn legend in the corner.
